title: 2026/02/18 - GA [Part 2]
displayTitle: 2026-02-18 - Governance and Administration Committee - Emergency Management Bill [Part 2]
description: Wednesday 18 February 2026 | Governance and Administration Committee Emergency Management Bill (No 2) Horizons Regional Council - Nickie Riley, Chair - Maria Reiche, Policy - Matthew Putt, EM Auckland Council - Alf Filipaina, Chair of Auckland Council CDEM Committee - Ken Turner, Deputy Chair of Auckland Council CDEM Committee - Michelle Wilson, Auckland Council CDEM Committee Houkura Representative - Adam Maggs, General Manager, Auckland Emergency Management - Sarah le Claire, Head of Strategy & Capability, Auckland Emergency Management - Wayne Brown, Principal Recovery Advisor, Auckland Emergency Management KiwiRail Holdings Limited - David Gordon, Chief Metro and Capital Programme Office - Marija Batistich, Senior Corporate Counsel
descriptionSummary: Emergency Management Bill. Emergency Management Bill (No 2). Horizons Regional Council

chunks.text: We'd just like to welcome the Horizons Regional Council, uh, who are joining us online to submit on the emergency management bill. Just see if we've got them there now. Hi. Um, it says that the host, oh, here we go. Hosts us Juststarting with you. Yep. Amazing.

We are running, running a bit early, but we're just checking to see if you're happy to do your submission now. Absolutely. Okay, fantastic. Um, we've got 10 minutes to hear from you. We've received a copy of your Russian submission, so you are welcome to use all of that time. We'll leave some time questions at the end, and apologies, um, you won't be able, there's many people sitting in the room, although you won't be able to see that, um, necessarily from the, from the image on Zoom.

So over to you. Okay, Thank you very much. Um, so I'm Sally Dryland and I am, uh, representing the Council Lu. Um, Nikki unfortunately is caught up with response from the last, uh, event. So, um, the event over the weekend and with me is, uh, Maria and Matt who, uh, offices.

So, uh, a region is large, uh, exposed to a range of natural hazards and is predominantly rural. And, um, is the regional Council where, um, the administrating authority for the Mantu Wgnu Civil Defense Emergency Management Group. Our submission focuses on strengthening the implementation to ensure the system works in practice, not just in stature.

We focus on clarity, integration and resourcing in the current environment with proposed rates keeping and concurrent reforms to resource management and local government. This creates a real implementation risk. Councils may be permitted to increase rates after a disaster, but not enabled to invest adequately in the preparedness beforehand.

We know disaster preparedness delivers a strong return. The RIS is estimated this at 4.1, uh, sorry, four to one, but there is still an upfront cost in our region. This is not theoretical. We are the only region in New Zealand without a rain radar, which significantly compromises our ability to prepare for short notice extreme weather events.

We strongly support strengthening Iwi and Hapu participation. The bill recognizes their value, but does not provide funding to support this participation in our region. Marai provide critical welfare support and emergencies. They should not be left carrying the financial burden in alignment with other major reforms.

The emergency management does not operate in isolation. Hazard responsibilities currently span the CDM Act, the RMA and the Local Government Act. These are all under reform as drafted. There are risk of inconsistent terminology, overlapping hazard planning functions and misalignment between regional plans, spatial plans, and the National Emergency management plan.

These inconsistencies create uncertainty for implementation. Flood protection infrastructure providers should be added to Schedule three as essential infrastructure and water service providers that provide only a minor aspect of water services should be excluded disproportionately affected.

Communities must be defined to ensure consistent identification of these communities and that addressing these their concerns sits at the appropriate level of government. Meaningful consultation, consultation timeframe should be specified at least 20 working days for national strategies, plans and regulations, arrangements for how offers of assistance will be managed.

And arra arrangements for animal welfare should be both set nationally. In closing, horizon supports the direction of reform. A region knows firsthand the importance of a strong, clear and coordinated emergency management system. Legislation alone will not strengthen the system.

Implementation depends on resourcing clarity and alignment with targeted amendments, particularly around funding, iwi participation and legislative integration. This bill could deliver a resilient, equitable and nationally consistent emergency management system.

This oral hearing on the emergency management bill, number two, Coursera. Um, I'm head of capability and strategy at Auckland Emergency Management, and I'm joined by Adam Mags, um, general manager and group chair, uh, group manager for, um, Auckland Emergency Management and also Wayne Brown, principal Recovery Advisor.

And, um, sitting in the corner is Jess Johnston, um, special Council for public law for any technical aspects as well. So you have our submission, as you've mentioned. Um, and our purpose today is to emphasize our overall support for the bill and draw the committee's attention to, uh, three technical aspects that particularly impact Auckland. So the bill reflects Auckland's own improvement pathway since the North island severe weather events of 2023 with tangible progress, um, um, in building our capability, our capacity, and our community connection.

And that's captured in our group plan and our subsequent reporting. Our submission states that Auckland Council broadly supports the bill and the need for a fully funded national improvement program. Our submission recommends several key refinements based on our lived experience in Auckland, and namely, clearer recognition of the crucial role of individuals, whanau, iwi, and communities.

A stronger positioning of iwi Maori as enduring partners within the emergency san uh, management system, enabling recovery to grow to the same level of maturity as response and addressing compliance costs and that risk of cost shifting to local government. The submission also recommends some technical amendments in support of our submission points, and I'll just elaborate on three of these.

Um, first of all, around the change definition of emergency, the council supports the updated definition of emergency, which now focuses on whether an event requires a significant coordinated response. We believe this wording will make it easier for decision makers to determine when the threshold for declaring a state of emergency has been met.

Um, we reflect that following the 2023 weather events, there has been strong public scrutiny around the timing of declarations, particularly in Auckland. There is now a greater understanding that a declaration is not only an operational tool, it also plays an important role in signaling the seriousness of an event, providing reassurance, and also helping the public respond appropriately.

Given this shift in expectations, Auckland Council would welcome updated national guidance. Consistent guidance would help ensure declarations are applied uniformly across the sector. And it would also be helpful for that guidance to clarify how far declarations should be used as a public information tool while still recognizing the extraordinary powers that accompany a state of emergency.

My second point relates to, um, who is authorized to declare a state of emergency, and this is a particularly Auckland, um, issue. So we would like to highlight that the provisions relating to who is authorized to declare a state of emergency, um, are unclear when applied to Auckland. And this relates to clauses 49 and 50, which set out two different pathways.

Clause 49 allows authorized persons appointed by the Emergency Management Committee to declare for whole areas multiple districts or multiple wards. While clause 50 gives mayors of territorial authorities the power to declare for individual districts or wards within their districts.

So Auckland, um, as you may be aware, does not have multiple districts. It's a unitary authority with a single district. As a result, it's unclear how Clause 50 is intended to operate here in Auckland. Specifically, we, um, request clarity on whether the Mayor of Auckland is empowered by Clause 50 part two for declarations affecting the whole Auckland District, or whether the mayor must instead be an appointed, be appointed as an authorized person under clause 49.

If clause 50 part two is intended to apply. It would also be helpful to clarify whether the hierarchy exists between whether a hierarchy exists between Clause 49 and clause 50 in determining who should make a declaration for the Auckland area. And additionally, based on current drafting, it appears that in a unitary, unitary authority context, where there's only one district that only the mayor is authorized to declare for a single ward, as there is no provision for an authorized person to do so in clause 49.

Um, that also, um, flows on, we believe into our, um, the authorities of our controllers, and we've set that out in recommendation three. So I won't repeat it here. Uh, and we have a final pro, uh, point that I'd like to raise around the provisions relating to lead agencies and share. We would like to raise an issue with the Bill's definition of lead agency.

The bill defines a lead agency as a government agency identified in the national emergency plan, because government agency is not defined, it is unclear how this applies in a local or regional emergency, or where the emergency management committee, so formally the Sedum group, has traditionally acted as the lead agency.

We are seeking clarification on a, a key point. Our emergency management committee still intended to be the lead agency for hazards such as severe weather, geological events or infrastructure failure. Or does the bill intend that the national level agency will always act as lead even for regional scale events? So if the intention is to shift lead agency responsibilities for local and regional emergencies to the national level, in all cases, that would be a significant departure from current practice and we would appreciate clarity on how

that would operate in real terms. And we therefore recommend that the bill confirm the status of lead agencies for local and regional emergencies and provide clear guidance on how these roles will function under the new framework. So that concludes our submission. Um, we welcome any questions you may have. Thank you so much for that detailed submission and for going through, um, all of the different, um, aspects of how it was approved as well.

It ended up being the, the, um, the minister who declared, um, emergencies in that situation. Be fair to say that a lot of the local councils weren't particularly pleased that that gap was there.
